title: World models for interactive theorem proving in Lean 4
description: Reframing ITP as model-based RL: learn a world model of Lean and plan with it (hierarchical MPC) at equal Lean-call budget. A port of HWM to Lean. Reasoning reconstructed from the mathematics-world-models repo; currently a data pipeline + a designed program.

glossary.world model (WM): A learned fast/approximate surrogate of the true Lean dynamics; predicts the next observation under an edit, so the planner can imagine edit sequences without calling the Lean kernel.
glossary.elementary perturbation: A single typed edit at one source site - the RL 'action'. Low-level primitives (INSERT/DELETE/REPLACE) vs high-level macro-edits (HAVE/SUFFICES/.../BLOCK).
glossary.HWM: Hierarchical Planning with Latent World Models (arXiv:2604.03208), the methodological template ported here: multi-scale latent world models + MPC, addressing compounding model error and exponential search.
glossary.observation (o_t): The canonical text block FILE || GOALS || MESSAGES || ENV || META that represents the Lean world-state for the model.
glossary.is_solved: Terminal predicate: GOALS == 'no goals' and no error messages and no sorry/admit.
glossary.Stages A/B/C/D: Data-pipeline stages: A forward expert transitions, B perturbed (failure + counterfactual), C macro (block-compression / structural), D round-trip forward/inverse consistency pairs.
glossary.grounding (receding horizon): Periodically executing the chosen edit prefix in the REAL Lean kernel and re-planning - the antidote to a world model's compounding prediction error.
glossary.equal-budget protocol: The fair-comparison currency: a fixed number of real Lean-kernel calls per theorem (imagined model steps are free). The isolation that makes the WM-vs-baseline comparison meaningful.
glossary.wm_low / wm_high: Two LoRA adapters on a shared frozen base model: the primitive-edit world model and the macro-edit world model.
glossary.L_cycle: Round-trip consistency loss on Stage-D invertible-operator pairs: applying an edit then its inverse should return the original state.
glossary.theorem-disjoint splits: Train/val/test split by declaration (sha256 of the decl name) so no theorem leaks across splits.
